narrative: I'm sorry to share that your law school application was rejected because our predictive model estimated a 41% chance that you might not pass the bar exam on your first try, which is above our threshold for approval. This prediction is based on your academic record, including your Year 1 school decile of 3—below the typical student's 5.71—and your Year 3 decile of 2, which is even lower than the average of 5.53. Your LSAT score of 29.5 also stands out as notably below the dataset average of 36.7, while your undergraduate GPA of 3.0 is slightly under the usual 3.21. Coming from a middle-income family, being a female applicant, identifying as White, and planning full-time enrollment align more closely with common profiles—White students make up about 84% of applicants, females around 44%, and full-time status over 92%—so these didn't sway the decision much. Among all these factors, your Year 3 decile had the strongest influence in pushing the model toward predicting a higher risk of bar failure, followed closely by your LSAT score, then your Year 1 decile, family income bracket, undergraduate GPA, racial background, gender, and full-time plans, in that order of impact. Each of these elements nudged the prediction slightly toward failure rather than success, with your lower academic deciles and LSAT carrying the most weight compared to typical students who tend to rank higher. In essence, while your profile matches many applicants demographically, your academic standings are what set you apart from those the model sees as safer bets for passing the bar. This doesn't reflect your full potential, just patterns from past students. To boost your chances next time, focus on strengthening your academic performance—perhaps by excelling in any remaining coursework or preparing rigorously for a potential LSAT retake, as those are areas where many students have improved outcomes. Building a stronger GPA through targeted study habits could also help shift future predictions in your favor. You're clearly motivated to pursue law, and with some focused effort on these key academic markers, you could align more closely with successful profiles. Keep pushing forward—we're rooting for you.
